sentences.base_language: Lara and her friend Diego are in the kitchen.
sentences.target_language: Lara y su amigo Diego están en la cocina.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Están** uses **estar** for location — where something physically is. **Ser** would be used for scheduled events at a location: **la clase es en la cocina** (the class is in the kitchen).
**Su amigo** = her friend (or his friend — **su** is ambiguous for gender of the possessor). Context clarifies: since Lara is the subject, **su** refers to her friend.

sentences.base_language: 'Today I'm going to teach you how to make arroz con leche,' says Diego.
sentences.target_language: —Hoy te voy a enseñar a hacer arroz con leche —dice Diego.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Te voy a enseñar** is ir a + infinitive for the near future: 'I'm going to teach you.' The indirect object **te** goes before the conjugated verb.
Alternatively it attaches to the infinitive: **voy a enseñarte** — both forms are equally correct.

sentences.base_language: 'Great! Tell me, what do we need?'
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dime** = tell me — the imperative of **decir** (to say/tell) with the reflexive/indirect pronoun **me** attached. Tú imperative of decir is **di** (irregular); attach **me**: **dime**.
Object pronouns always attach to the end of affirmative commands: **dime**, **tráeme** (bring me), **ayúdame** (help me). They go before negative commands: **no me digas** (don't tell me).

sentences.base_language: 'We need rice, milk, sugar, cinnamon, and a little lemon zest.'
sentences.target_language: —Necesitamos arroz, leche, azúcar, canela y un poco de ralladura de limón.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Azúcar** (sugar) comes from Arabic *al-sukkar*, which itself came from Sanskrit *sharkara*. One of many Arabic-origin words in Spanish food vocabulary.
**Un poco de ralladura de limón** = a little lemon zest. **Ralladura** comes from **rallar** (to grate/zest) — note the double **ll**. **De** links the material to the noun: ralladura de limón (zest of lemon).

sentences.base_language: 'First, measure two cups of rice and rinse it well.'
sentences.target_language: —Primero, mide dos tazas de arroz y enjuágalo bien.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mide** and **enjuágalo** are tú imperatives (informal commands). -ir verbs like medir use the él/ella form: mide. The -lo in enjuágalo is a direct object pronoun attached to the command.
Attaching pronouns to commands is standard: dámelo (give it to me), ponlo (put it).

sentences.base_language: 'Now add the milk and cook on low heat.'
sentences.target_language: —Ahora añade la leche y cocina a fuego lento.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Fuego lento** (low heat) literally means 'slow fire.' Also used figuratively to mean 'slowly' or 'gradually.'
fuego alto (high heat), fuego medio (medium heat), fuego lento (low heat)

**Lo revuelvo** = do I stir it? **Lo** is the direct object pronoun (it, masc.), placed before the conjugated verb.
**Revolver** (to stir) is an o→ue stem-changing verb: revuelvo, revuelves, revuelve. Literally 'to turn around/mix' — same root as **volver** (to return) and **revolver** (the firearm, which 'revolves').

sentences.base_language: 'Yes, stir it often so it doesn't stick to the bottom.'
sentences.target_language: —Sí, revuélvelo seguido para que no se pegue al fondo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para que no se pegue** = so that it doesn't stick — **para que + subjunctive** expresses purpose with a different subject. Whenever **para que** introduces a clause with a new subject, the subjunctive is required: **pegue** (present subjunctive of **pegar**).
**Al fondo** = **a + el fondo** — mandatory contraction. **Al fondo** means 'at/to the bottom.'

**Le agrego** = do I add it to it? The **le** here is an indirect object pronoun referring to the arroz con leche (the dish being cooked). Spanish frequently uses indirect object pronouns to indicate the recipient/container even when it's already named: **le pongo sal al arroz** (I add salt to the rice).
**¿Cuándo...?** — question words in Spanish always carry an accent mark: **cuándo**, **qué**, **cómo**, **dónde**, **quién**, **cuánto**.

sentences.base_language: 'Add it after fifteen minutes, when the rice is soft. Then sprinkle some cinnamon on top.'
sentences.target_language: —Agrégalo a los quince minutos, cuando el arroz esté blando. Luego espolvorea canela por encima.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cuando el arroz esté blando** — **cuando** (when) referring to a future or uncertain event triggers the **subjunctive**: **esté** (present subjunctive of **estar**). If referring to a habitual or past event, use indicative: **cuando el arroz estaba blando** (when the rice was soft).
**Agrégalo** and **espolvorea** are tú imperatives with pronoun attached. **Espolvorear** (to sprinkle/dust) comes from **polvo** (dust/powder) — also gives **empolvar** (to powder) and **pólvora** (gunpowder).

sentences.base_language: Lara stirs the pot and the kitchen fills with a sweet, warm aroma.
sentences.target_language: Lara revuelve la olla y la cocina se llena de un aroma dulce y cálido.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se llena de** = fills with (reflexive construction). **Llenarse de** means 'to fill up with' — the reflexive **se** signals that the subject undergoes the action rather than performing it on something else.
**Cálido** = warm (emotionally warm or comfortably warm). Compare **caliente** (hot to the touch): **agua caliente** (hot water) vs. **un abrazo cálido** (a warm hug).

sentences.base_language: 'It smells incredible! I think I'm going to make this every week.'
sentences.target_language: —¡Huele increíble! Creo que voy a hacer esto todas las semanas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Creo que** + indicative = I think that... (personal opinion or belief).
**Espero que** + subjunctive = I hope that... Use creo que when stating what you think is true; espero que when expressing hope or uncertainty.
